meta.title: FlowMind WASM Kernel — Full Benchmark Report
meta.description: 10-model exhaustive evaluation across 8 task categories. WASM kernel + local LLM + browser inference architecture.

props.title: FlowMind WASM Kernel
props.subtitle: Four-Tier JIT Execution — From 11µs to 3.9s, Every Iota of Inference Mapped

props.content: 10 models tested. 18 tasks across 8 categories. 54% of FlowMind expressions now run in WASM at 11µs. The remaining 46% route through a tiered LLM stack — from 130MB browser models at 36ms to 8B Bonsai at 3.9s. Nothing counted out. Every model has a use case.

props.label: ARCHITECTURE
props.title: Four-Tier JIT Execution Model
props.subtitle: Every instruction token routes to the cheapest tier that can resolve it

props.content:
graph TD
    A[Text Stream with Tokens] --> B{Parse Bindings}
    B --> C["{{var.path}}"]
    B --> D["[[scope:action]]"]
    B --> E["lev://module"]
    C --> F{Tier Classifier}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Expression| G[T0: WASM Kernel 11µs]
    F -->|Semantic| H{Server Available?}
    H -->|Yes| I[T1: Ollama/Bonsai 100ms-4s]
    H -->|No| J{WebGPU Available?}
    J -->|Yes + Opted-in| K[T0.5: Browser LLM 200-500ms]
    J -->|No| L[T2: Frontier LLM 2-5s]
    G --> M[Resolved Binding]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Host Adapter}
    N -->|CLI| O[argv / env / files]
    N -->|Browser| P[DOM / Agent Ping]
    N -->|Desktop| Q[Native controls]
    N -->|Remote| R[Forwarded agent]

props.label: WASM KERNEL
props.title: lev-kernel-wasm — 33 Tests, 297KB
props.subtitle: Deterministic expression evaluation ported from TypeScript to Rust

props.label: BENCHMARK RESULTS
props.title: 10-Model × 8-Category Matrix
props.subtitle: Every model tested on 18 tasks across boolean, routing, safety, completion, validation, sentiment, keyword, comparison

props.caption: Accuracy by Category (% correct) — sorted by total accuracy

props.label: BONSAI DEEP DIVE
props.title: Bonsai-8B — 1-Bit Quantized, 72% Accuracy
props.subtitle: PrismML's end-to-end 1-bit model, 14.1× smaller than FP16, running through Ollama Q4_K_M

props.content:
Bonsai-8B is PrismML's end-to-end 1-bit language model based on Qwen3 architecture. Each weight is a single bit (0 → −scale, 1 → +scale) with one FP16 scale per 128 weights. The native GGUF Q1_0 format is 1.15GB — 14.2× smaller than FP16.

In our benchmark, Bonsai ties phi4-mini at 72% overall accuracy and is the **only model to score 100% on boolean tasks** (yes/no questions). It also achieves 100% on sentiment, keyword detection, and comparison.

The 3.9s average latency is due to Qwen3's built-in thinking mode — the model generates a `<think>` block before answering. Through the PrismML native llama.cpp fork with Metal kernels, this would be ~6× faster (~650ms). The native 1-bit GGUF could not load in standard Ollama (Q1_0 requires custom kernels), so we tested the bartowski Q4_K_M standard quantization (5.76GB).

**Key insight:** Bonsai's thinking overhead is a feature, not a bug — it produces more accurate answers on complex tasks. For gate classification, a `/no_think` prompt prefix or thinking-token stripping gives the best latency/accuracy tradeoff.

props.label: PER-CATEGORY ANALYSIS
props.title: Best Model Per Task Type
props.subtitle: Every iota of inference has a use case

props.title: 🎯 Boolean (yes/no)
props.content:
**Winner: Bonsai-8B + TinyLlama (100%)**

Simplest inference — 'is X > Y?', 'is this empty?'. Even 135M models score 67%. TinyLlama nails all 3 at 306ms avg. Bonsai at 2.4s.

*Browser-viable:* TinyLlama at 637MB download.

props.title: 🔀 Routing (classify)
props.content:
**Winner: phi4-mini + gemma3:1b (100%)**

Classify tasks into 2-3 buckets. 'Code agent vs research agent?', 'bug fix or feature?'. gemma3:1b achieves 100% at 279ms with only 815MB.

*Browser-viable:* gemma3:1b is the sweet spot.

props.title: 🛡️ Safety (vulnerability)
props.content:
**Winner: Nobody (33% across the board)**

Every model struggles with SQL injection, XSS, and input sanitization detection. This is a **frontier LLM task** — even 8B models can't reliably detect code vulnerabilities.

*Implication:* Safety gates MUST use T2 frontier models.

props.title: 😊 Sentiment (pos/neg)
props.content:
**Winner: SmolLM2-360M (100% at 36ms)**

The easiest task for any model above 360M. Five models score 100%. SmolLM2-360M does it at 36ms — the fastest by far.

*Browser-viable:* SmolLM2-360M (130MB) is perfect for this.

props.title: 🔑 Keyword Detection
props.content:
**Winner: qwen2.5:1.5b + phi4-mini (100%)**

'Does this mention authentication?' — concept detection in text. Needs ~1.5B+ params to reliably distinguish concepts.

*Browser-viable:* qwen2.5:1.5b at 986MB.

props.title: ⚖️ Comparison (A vs B)
props.content:
**Winner: 7 models tie at 100%**

Almost universally solvable — 'which is more maintainable?'. Only TinyLlama and qwen3:4b fail.

*Browser-viable:* SmolLM2-135M (60MB) scores 100%.

props.title: ✅ Validation (schema)
props.content:
**Winner: SmolLM2-360M (50% at 36ms)**

'Does this have required fields?' — detecting missing schema fields. No model exceeds 50%. Negative validation (detecting MISSING fields) is hard for small models.

*Implication:* Combine WASM (deterministic field check) + LLM (semantic validation).

props.title: 🏁 Completion (is it done?)
props.content:
**Winner: SmolLM2-360M (50% at 56ms)**

'Is the migration complete?' — models reliably detect 'done' but struggle with 'not done' when files remain. The positive bias is strong in small models.

*Implication:* Completion detection needs structured checks, not just LLM.

props.label: BROWSER LLM
props.title: Tier 0.5 — In-Browser Inference via WebGPU
props.subtitle: User opts in to download a model package, runs entirely client-side

props.title: 1. Detect
props.content: Check `navigator.gpu` for WebGPU support. Check IndexedDB for cached model.

props.title: 2. Consent
props.content: Show download size. User clicks to opt in. Background Fetch API starts resumable download.

props.title: 3. Cache
props.content: Model cached in IndexedDB. Works offline for all future sessions.

props.title: 4. Infer
props.content: WebGPU compute shaders run gate evaluation at 200-500ms. Falls through to T1/T2 on failure.

props.title: Accuracy (%) vs Download Size (MB) — sorted by accuracy
Pareto Frontier
props.content: phi4-mini (72% @ 208ms, 2.5GB) dominates the frontier. For browser deployment, SmolLM2-360M (56% @ 92ms, 130MB) is the best bang-per-megabyte. Bonsai-8B ties phi4-mini on accuracy but at 16× the latency through Ollama — native 1-bit inference would close that gap to ~2-3×.

props.title: Which Model For Which Job?
props.subtitle: The right model for every iota of inference

items.title: Meridian Intake + WASM Kernel v1
items.description: Ingested Meridian paper. Built lev-kernel-wasm in Rust: gate evaluator, constraint manifold, graph ops. 19 tests, 297KB.

items.title: Parser Extension + 5 Spikes
items.description: Added ==, !=, contains, {{var}}, ${var}}. Coverage 0% → 52%. JIT benchmark: 62.8% WASM-eligible, 67K× speedup. Execution IR DNA drafted.

items.title: Browser LLM Research + Small Model Bench
items.description: WebLLM, Transformers.js v4, Chrome Built-in AI research. phi4-mini 80%, llama3.2:1b 20%. OR/AND/in[] added. Coverage → 54%, 33 tests.

items.title: Mega-Benchmark + Bonsai
items.description: Pulled 6 new models. 10-model × 18-task × 8-category mega-bench. Bonsai-8B imported and tested: 72% accuracy, 100% boolean.

items.title: Cross-Platform Showcase
items.description: WASM kernel + WebLLM in Agent Ping extension. Live demo: gate evaluation on a real website. Autoresearch 20-tick prompt optimization.

items.title: Native 1-Bit Bonsai + PLC Integration
items.description: PrismML fork for native Q1_0 inference. Embedded systems (PLC). Remote agent topology. Full FlowMind JIT compiler.

props.content: The WASM kernel eliminates 54% of all FlowMind evaluations at 11µs. The remaining 46% are prose conditions that require inference — but not all inference is equal.

props.title: Insight 1: Small Models Have Real Use Cases
props.content: SmolLM2-360M (130MB) achieves 100% on sentiment and comparison at 36ms. That's a browser-downloadable model that can reliably answer 'is this positive or negative?' and 'which of A/B is better?' — two of the most common gate predicates in content workflows.

props.title: Insight 2: Safety Is a Frontier-Only Task
props.content: Every model scores exactly 33% on safety (vulnerability detection). SQL injection, XSS, and input sanitization require reasoning about code semantics that sub-8B models cannot do. This validates the four-tier architecture — safety gates MUST route to T2.

props.title: Insight 3: Thinking Models Need Special Handling
props.content: Qwen3-based models (qwen3:4b, bonsai-8b) produce <think> blocks before answering. With max_tokens=10, the answer gets cut off. With think-stripping and max_tokens=256, Bonsai achieves 72% — tying phi4-mini. The thinking overhead adds ~2-3s but improves accuracy on harder tasks.

props.title: Insight 4: The Execution Topology Is Host-Independent
props.content: The instruction graph (what to resolve) is stable. The execution topology (where to resolve it) changes per deployment: CLI uses Ollama, browser uses WebLLM + IndexedDB cache, Agent Ping extension ships the 297KB WASM kernel plus an optional model download, remote agents forward the same plan with different credentials. One IR, many hosts.
